text: This is a case of a patient who was having pain for two days and came to the hospital where a CT scan was performed and showed an acute perforation of an ulcer. So we brought her emergently to the OR. Luckily, where I practice, I'm able to utilize the robot at all hours of the day. So even though this was a midnight case, I was able to successfully treat her. As you can see as we entered there was bile flowing everywhere and pus and abscesses starting to form. Likely this had perforated over a day ago and she had been sitting at home in pain. But she came to the hospital here and we were able to get her added on.

The surgeon elevates the liver to inspect the stomach for gastric perforation, finding it intact. The gallbladder is lifted, revealing a large ulcer on the lateral wall of the duodenum with food particles present. The surgeon decides on primary closure using a 2-0 V-lock suture, running it to close the ulcer with minimal tension on the duodenal wall.

The surgeon uses scissors to divide adhesions and free a vascularized tongue of omentum, creating an omental patch. The patch is sutured in place with interrupted silk sutures, laying smoothly over the closure without tension. The surgical field shows no bleeding or leaks.
